## (a) Implementation

```dafny
function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id
{
  if x.width >= PAD then x
  else Id(x.kind, x.value, PAD)
}
```

## (b) Lemma specification

```dafny
lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind          // kind never changes
  ensures Canonicalize(x).value == x.value        // numeric value never changes
  ensures Canonicalize(x).width == if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD
                                                  // width = max(x.width, PAD); >=PAD unchanged
  ensures Wellformed(Canonicalize(x))             // result is wellformed
  ensures Canonicalize(x).width >= PAD            // result has at least minimum width
  ensures Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)  // idempotent (no-op on canonical)
{ }
```

### Notes on completeness

- **Kind & value preserved** (`.kind`, `.value`): guarantees the id still refers to the same entity.
- **Width formula** (`if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD`): this is exactly `max(x.width, PAD)`. It captures both:
  - ids already at width ≥ 4 are returned *unchanged* (so an already-canonical id maps to itself);
  - smaller widths are padded *up to* the minimum 4.
- **`width >= PAD`**: the output is always at the canonical minimum width.
- **`Wellformed(Canonicalize(x))`**: the chosen width can still hold the value (follows since width ≥ original width ≥ NumDigits(value), but stated for completeness).
- **Idempotence**: canonicalizing a canonical id is a no-op.

The width clause alone pins the result down uniquely given the value/kind clauses, so this specification is both precise (no implementation other than the intended one satisfies it) and complete.
